name: Mayahuel
description: Mayahuel is the Aztec goddess of maguey (agave) and pulque, an alcoholic beverage made from the fermented sap of the maguey plant. She is often depicted with many breasts, symbolizing her role as a nurturer and provider of sustenance. According to myth, Mayahuel was a beautiful maiden who lived with her grandmother, a fearsome Tzitzimitl demon. Quetzalcoatl, the feathered serpent god, fell in love with her and convinced her to flee with him to the earth. To escape her grandmother's wrath, they transformed themselves into a forked tree. However, the grandmother found them and tore the tree apart, killing Mayahuel. Quetzalcoatl gathered her bones and buried them, from which the first maguey plant sprang. This act connected her directly to the plant and its intoxicating drink, making her a symbol of fertility, nourishment, and revelry. She is also associated with rabbits, often depicted with them, and is considered a patron of healers and midwives. Her worship involved rituals centered around pulque and the maguey plant, celebrating abundance and the cyclical nature of life and death. Mayahuel represents the life-giving properties of the earth and the intoxicating power of transformation.
subtitle: Goddess of Maguey and Pulque

keyMyths.title: The Flight with Quetzalcoatl
keyMyths.description: Mayahuel flees with Quetzalcoatl, transforming into a tree to escape her grandmother. She is killed, and Quetzalcoatl plants her bones, creating the maguey.

keyMyths.title: The Origin of Pulque
keyMyths.description: Mayahuel's association with the maguey plant led to her being revered as the goddess of pulque. Pulque was used in rituals and celebrations.

keyMyths.title: Mayahuel and Rabbits
keyMyths.description: Mayahuel is often depicted with rabbits, associating her with fertility and abundance. The rabbits may also represent the intoxicating effects of pulque.

sources.author: Sahagún, Bernardino de
sources.description: A primary source describing Aztec culture and religion.

worshipPractices.name: Pulque Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Pulque, the fermented beverage derived from the maguey plant, was a primary offering to Mayahuel. It was consumed during rituals and festivals to honor the goddess and seek her blessings for fertility, abundance, and intoxication.

worshipPractices.name: Maguey Plant Rituals
worshipPractices.description: Rituals involving the maguey plant itself were performed to honor Mayahuel. These could include planting ceremonies, harvesting rituals, and the careful extraction of the plant's sap, all performed with reverence for the goddess's life-giving properties.

festivals.name: Tecuilhuitontli
festivals.timing: The eighth month of the Aztec calendar
festivals.description: Tecuilhuitontli, meaning 'little feast of the lords,' was a festival dedicated to various deities, including Mayahuel. It involved feasting, dancing, and the consumption of pulque, celebrating the abundance provided by the gods and goddesses.

cultural.modernLegacy: While direct worship of Mayahuel is no longer practiced, her image and symbolism persist in Mexican culture. She is often depicted in art and craft, representing fertility, nourishment, and the cultural significance of the maguey plant and pulque in Mexican heritage. The traditions surrounding agave plants and their use in beverages like tequila also subtly echo Mayahuel's ancient role.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Mayahuel's origins are rooted in the Aztec understanding of the life cycle and the gifts of the natural world. She is considered an embodiment of the maguey plant, a vital resource for the Aztecs, providing food, fiber, and the key ingredient for pulque. Myths recount that Mayahuel was a beautiful maiden, hidden away by her grandmother, a fearsome Tzitzimitl demon, to protect her from the world. Some accounts describe her as one of many goddesses associated with fertility and the earth's bounty. Her connection to the Tzitzimimeh suggests a link to the stars and the celestial realm, further emphasizing the interconnectedness of the natural and supernatural worlds in Aztec cosmology. Quetzalcoatl's intervention in her life marks a significant turning point, leading to the discovery of pulque and its introduction to humanity.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: The most prominent myth involving Mayahuel tells of her encounter with Quetzalcoatl. Desiring to share Mayahuel's beauty and the maguey's gifts with humanity, Quetzalcoatl sought her out. They fell in love and fled together, transforming themselves into a tree with two branches to escape Mayahuel's grandmother. However, the grandmother discovered them and tore down Mayahuel's branch, killing her. Quetzalcoatl, grief-stricken, buried her remains, from which the first maguey plant sprouted. He then learned how to extract the plant's sap and ferment it into pulque, sharing this gift with humankind. This myth highlights themes of love, sacrifice, and the transformation of loss into sustenance. It also emphasizes Quetzalcoatl's role as a benefactor of humanity, bringing them the gifts of agriculture and civilization.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Worship of Mayahuel centered around the maguey plant and its products, particularly pulque. Pulque was not merely a beverage; it was a sacred substance consumed during religious ceremonies and festivals. It was believed to possess spiritual properties and was offered to the gods, including Mayahuel, as a form of tribute and supplication. Priests and priestesses likely played a role in these rituals, overseeing the preparation and distribution of pulque. The Tecuilhuitontli festival was particularly important, celebrating the abundance provided by the gods and featuring the consumption of pulque. Mayahuel's association with fertility also meant that she was likely invoked in rituals related to childbirth and the well-being of children. Her multi-breasted depiction reinforces her role as a nurturing and life-sustaining deity.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Although the Aztec empire fell to Spanish conquistadors, Mayahuel's influence can still be observed in modern Mexican culture. Her image continues to appear in art, representing the country's rich cultural heritage and the importance of the maguey plant. The production and consumption of tequila and mezcal, both derived from agave plants, can be seen as a continuation of the traditions associated with Mayahuel. While direct worship has ceased, the reverence for the agave plant and its products remains a significant part of Mexican identity. Mayahuel serves as a reminder of the Aztecs' deep connection to the natural world and their understanding of the sacredness of sustenance. She embodies the life-giving forces of nature and the transformative power of sacrifice, continuing to resonate with people today.

shortDescription: Aztec goddess of the maguey plant and pulque, a fermented beverage. She represents fertility, nourishment, and the sacredness of sustenance.
